title: He Kōrero Mō Te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Sustainable Storytelling for Our Waterways
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional story) of Hinepūkohurangi and her connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe. They will investigate current water quality issues affecting our awa while learning how storytelling serves as both cultural preservation and environmental advocacy within Ngāti Kahungunu.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional Māori pūrākau with contemporary environmental reporting to identify narrative techniques used in both forms
Create a persuasive multimedia text (video or digital story) that advocates for the protection of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using specific linguistic and visual features
Collaborate with peers to gather and synthesize community perspectives on the health of our local waterway
Apply understanding of text structure and language features to effectively communicate sustainability solutions to authentic audiences

activities.title: He Timatanga: The Story of Our Awa
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua share the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and her connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Through guided discussion, they identify the narrative structure, symbolic language, and environmental values embedded in the traditional story.

activities.title: Te Awa Ināianei: Current State Investigation
activities.description: Students examine recent water quality data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council for Mangakōtukutuku Stream, then conduct field observations along designated safe access points. They document their observations through descriptive writing and photography, noting both natural beauty and potential environmental concerns.

activities.title: Ngā Reo o Te Kāinga: Community Voices
activities.description: In small groups, students develop interview questions and practice active listening techniques before conducting brief interviews with family/community members about their connections to and concerns for the local waterway. They transcribe key quotes and identify common themes.

activities.title: He Korowai Mō Te Awa: Creating Our Protective Cloak
activities.description: Using digital storytelling tools, students create 2-3 minute multimedia presentations that weave together the traditional pūrākau, current environmental data, and community voices to advocate for specific actions to protect Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

activities.title: Whakanuia: Sharing Our Stories
activities.description: Students present their multimedia stories to an audience that includes classmates, teachers, and invited community members. They practice effective presentation skills and respond to questions about their creative choices and advocacy messages.

resources: Local kaumātua knowledgeable about Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau and Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality data and reports for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Digital storytelling tools (Canva, Adobe Spark, or WeVideo) and recording equipment
'Te Awa' by Rebecca Priestley and 'The River Talks' by local Hawke's Bay writers

tasks: Multimedia advocacy presentation (2-3 minutes) that integrates traditional storytelling, environmental data, and community perspectives
Written reflection (300-400 words) explaining linguistic and structural choices made in their presentation
Peer feedback on two other presentations using specific success criteria

nzcAlignment: English: Processes and Strategies - Integrating sources of information, processes, and strategies to identify, form, and express ideas
English: Ideas - Show an increasing understanding of ideas within, across, and beyond texts
English: Language Features - Use a range of language features appropriately, showing an increasing understanding of their effects
English: Structure - Organize texts, using a range of appropriate structures
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
